title: Operations Strategy
simple_html:
Operations strategy is the set of long-term decisions that shape how an operation supports the company's overall goals. Strategy in general is about choosing where to compete and how to win; operations strategy is about building the production and delivery system that actually delivers on that promise.

The core idea

A great business idea fails if operations can't deliver it. If a firm promises the cheapest product, its operations must be ruthlessly efficient. If it promises the most innovative product, its operations must be flexible. Operations strategy aligns what the operation is good at with what the market wants.

Two directions of fit

  • Top-down: the corporate and business strategy sets the direction, and operations follows it.
  • Bottom-up: day-to-day operational experience reveals new capabilities that can shape future strategy.
  • Market requirements: what customers want (their order-winners) pulls operations into shape.
  • Operations resources: what the operation is uniquely good at can become a competitive advantage.

The best strategy keeps all four in balance, this is called achieving fit.

Key takeaways

  • Operations strategy connects the boardroom's goals to the shop floor's actions.
  • It works in two directions: strategy guides operations, and operations capabilities inform strategy.
  • It must balance what the market wants with what the operation can do.
  • Good strategy makes deliberate choices and trade-offs, you cannot be best at everything for everyone.
intermediate_html:
Operations strategy is the pattern of decisions and actions that shapes the long-term capabilities of an operation and its contribution to overall strategy. Where Lesson 2 gave us the performance objectives (quality, speed, dependability, flexibility, cost), operations strategy is about deciding which of these to prioritise and how to build the operation to deliver them.

The four perspectives on operations strategy

Slack and Lewis describe operations strategy as the reconciliation of four perspectives:

  • Top-down: operations strategy should translate and support the business and corporate strategy. The hierarchy runs corporate strategy then business strategy then functional (operations) strategy.
  • Bottom-up: real operational experience accumulates into capabilities that feed up and reshape strategy, the idea of emergent strategy (Mintzberg).
  • Market requirements (outside-in): the strategy is pulled by what customers value, expressed as order-winners and qualifiers (Hill).
  • Operations resources (inside-out): the strategy is pushed by the firm's distinctive resources and capabilities, the resource-based view (Barney, Wernerfelt).

A coherent operations strategy reconciles all four: market requirements and operations resources must meet (this meeting point is called fit), and top-down intent must connect with bottom-up reality.

The concept of fit

Fit means the operation's capabilities match the market's requirements at an appropriate level. Too little capability for a demanding market means lost customers; lavish capability for an undemanding market wastes resources. Strategy aims to move the line of fit outward over time, raising capability and serving more demanding markets profitably.

Decision areas

Operations strategy is implemented through choices in two groups of decision areas:

  • Structural (hard to reverse, capital-heavy): capacity, supply network/vertical integration, process technology, and facilities.
  • Infrastructural (softer, ongoing): planning and control systems, quality systems, organisation, and improvement processes.

These are sometimes labelled the operations strategy content; the process of operations strategy is how the strategy is actually formulated and implemented.

Worked example

Consider IKEA. Its business strategy is "democratic design": good design at low prices. Reading the four perspectives: market requirements are low cost and acceptable quality/style (cost is the order-winner). Operations resources are flat-pack design, self-service warehouses, and global sourcing scale, distinctive capabilities competitors struggle to copy. Top-down, every operational choice supports low cost. Bottom-up, decades of flat-pack experience created capabilities that now shape what IKEA can promise. The decision areas all align: huge low-cost out-of-town facilities (structural), simple pull-based replenishment (infrastructural), and customers performing assembly themselves. The fit is tight, which is exactly why IKEA is hard to imitate.

How to apply it

To build or audit an operations strategy: (1) identify the market's order-winners and qualifiers, (2) assess the operation's current resources and capabilities, (3) check for fit, where are capabilities below or above what the market needs?, (4) work through each structural and infrastructural decision area and ask whether it supports the priority objectives, and (5) sequence improvements so capabilities are built cumulatively (the sand-cone logic from Lesson 2). The output is a coherent set of decisions where every operational choice pulls in the same strategic direction. Incoherence, e.g. a cost strategy undermined by an expensive flexible process, is the classic failure mode.
full_html:
Operations strategy is the total pattern of decisions that shapes the long-term capabilities of an operation and their contribution to the organisation's overall strategy, through the reconciliation of market requirements with operations resources (Slack and Lewis, 2017). It is the bridge between Lesson 2's performance objectives and the design lessons that follow: strategy decides which objectives matter and how the operation should be configured to deliver them. The field rests on a deceptively simple but historically neglected claim, made forcefully by Skinner (1969) in "Manufacturing, missing link in corporate strategy": that operations is a strategic, not merely tactical, function and must be designed in deliberate alignment with competitive intent.

Foundations: from neutral to supportive

Hayes and Wheelwright's (1984) four-stage model charts how operations can contribute strategically. Stage 1 (internally neutral) treats operations as a necessary evil to be minimised; Stage 2 (externally neutral) merely matches industry practice; Stage 3 (internally supportive) makes operations consistent with the business strategy; and Stage 4 (externally supportive) makes operations a source of competitive advantage, where strategy is built on operational capability. The strategic ambition of operations strategy is to reach Stage 4, the position occupied by firms like Toyota, IKEA, and Amazon whose advantage is their operating system.

The four perspectives

Slack and Lewis frame operations strategy as the reconciliation of four perspectives, two concerning direction (top-down vs bottom-up) and two concerning the basis of advantage (market vs resources):

  • Top-down: operations strategy as a deliberate translation of corporate and business strategy down the hierarchy, the classic planning view (Andrews; Porter, 1985, on generic strategies of cost leadership vs differentiation).
  • Bottom-up: strategy as emergent (Mintzberg and Waters, 1985), in which operational experience and learning accumulate into capabilities that feed upward and reshape intended strategy.
  • Market requirements (outside-in): the operation shaped by what customers value, formalised through Hill's (1985) order-winners and qualifiers. The order-winners define which performance objectives the operation must excel at.
  • Operations resources (inside-out): the resource-based view (Wernerfelt, 1984; Barney, 1991), in which sustainable advantage flows from resources and capabilities that are valuable, rare, inimitable, and organisationally embedded (the VRIO test). Operations capabilities, being tacit and systemic, are often the hardest of all resources to imitate.

A robust operations strategy reconciles all four; the meeting of market requirements and operations resources is the central act of fit.

Fit, sustainability, and the line of fit

Slack and Lewis model fit as the alignment between the level of operations capability and the level of market requirements along a diagonal "line of fit." Below the line, capability lags the market and customers defect; above it, capability is wasted on an undemanding market. Strategy seeks not only to reach the line but to move it outward, raising capability to serve ever more demanding markets, while managing the risk of being off the line during transitions. Porter's (1996) argument that "strategy is about making trade-offs" and creating "fit among activities" reinforces this: sustainable advantage comes from a tightly fitting system of operational choices that rivals cannot cherry-pick, not from any single best practice.

Decision areas: the content of operations strategy

Strategy is enacted through choices in defined decision areas, conventionally split into structural and infrastructural (Hayes and Wheelwright, 1984):

TypeDecision areaIllustrative question
Structural (hard, capital-intensive, slow to change)CapacityHow much, when, of what scale, and where? (Lesson 5)
Supply network / vertical integrationMake or buy; how much of the chain to own? (Lesson 6)
Process technologyWhich transformation technologies and how automated? (Lesson 4)
FacilitiesLocation, layout, and focus of sites? (Lesson 4)
Infrastructural (soft, behavioural, ongoing)Planning and controlHow is work scheduled and inventory managed? (Lesson 5)
QualityHow is conformance assured and improved? (Lesson 8)
ImprovementHow does the operation get better over time? (Lessons 7-8)
Organisation / human resourcesHow are people structured, skilled, and motivated?

The distinction between strategy content (what the decisions are) and strategy process (how the strategy is formulated, communicated, and implemented) is important: many sound strategies fail not in content but in a flawed process of engagement and rollout.

The trade-off question revisited

Operations strategy inherits the trade-off debate from Lesson 2. The orthodox view (Skinner; Porter, 1996) holds that strategy requires deliberate trade-offs, a focused operation deliberately gives up some objectives to excel at others. The capabilities/Japanese-manufacturing view (Ferdows and De Meyer's 1990 sand-cone model) holds that, over time and through cumulative capability-building (quality then dependability then speed then cost), apparent trade-offs can be relaxed. Both are correct at different horizons: trade-offs bind a fixed operation in the short run, but strategic investment in capability can shift the trade-off curve outward in the long run. Mature operations strategy therefore manages both the positioning (which trade-offs to make now) and the development (which capabilities to build for later).

Worked example

Apply the framework to Zara (Inditex). Market requirements: fast fashion, the order-winners are speed and flexibility (newness on the shelf), with cost and quality as qualifiers, the opposite of IKEA. Operations resources: vertically integrated, geographically proximate manufacturing in Spain/Portugal, twice-weekly replenishment, and small-batch production, a tightly fitting system of capabilities. Top-down: the business strategy of responsive fashion dictates these choices. Bottom-up: store-level sales data continuously feeds design, an emergent, learning-driven loop. Decision areas: deliberately near-shored facilities and higher production cost (structural) are accepted to win on responsiveness; pull-based planning and control (infrastructural) sustain it. Zara consciously trades the lowest unit cost (it could outsource to Asia more cheaply) for speed, illustrating Porter's point that strategy is about what you choose not to do. The fit among these choices is what makes the model durable.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Treating operations strategy as cost-cutting. It is about aligning capabilities to chosen order-winners, which may be speed or flexibility, not cost.
  • Pursuing best practice indiscriminately. Adopting a rival's lean or six-sigma toolkit without regard to your own market requirements imports tools without fit (Porter, 1996, distinguishes operational effectiveness from strategy).
  • Ignoring the bottom-up/resource perspective. Purely top-down strategy misses the inimitable capabilities that emerge from operational learning.
  • Incoherent decision areas. A cost strategy supported by an expensive flexible process, or a responsiveness strategy crippled by distant low-cost sourcing, destroys fit, the most common real-world failure.
  • Confusing content with process. A brilliant strategy on paper fails if the formulation/implementation process does not engage the operation.

Cross-links

Operations strategy is the organising spine of the course. It consumes Lesson 2's performance objectives and order-winners as inputs, and it dictates the downstream design decisions: process type and layout (Lesson 4) follow from the product-process matrix implied by the chosen objectives; capacity, inventory, and planning-and-control (Lesson 5) are core structural and infrastructural decision areas; supply-chain configuration (Lesson 6) is the supply-network decision; and the improvement philosophies of lean (Lesson 7) and quality/six sigma (Lesson 8) are how the operation moves its line of fit outward over time. Mastering this lesson lets you read every later lesson as an instance of a strategic decision area.
